title: Big-O Notation
definition: Big-O notation describes the upper bound on how an algorithm's running time or space grows as the input size increases, ignoring constants and lower-order terms.
importance: It is the shared language for comparing algorithm efficiency and is asked in virtually every technical interview.

title: Space Complexity
definition: Space complexity measures the total amount of memory an algorithm needs relative to its input size, including auxiliary space and recursion stack.
importance: Understanding space trade-offs lets you choose algorithms that fit memory-constrained environments.

title: Time Complexity
definition: Time complexity expresses how the number of basic operations an algorithm performs scales with the size of its input.
importance: It is the primary tool for predicting whether an algorithm will run fast enough at scale.

title: Recursion & Backtracking
definition: Recursion solves a problem by having a function call itself on smaller inputs; backtracking extends this by undoing choices that fail to reach a valid solution.
importance: They underpin tree/graph traversal, combinatorial search, and many divide-and-conquer algorithms.

title: Master Theorem
definition: The Master Theorem provides a formula to solve recurrence relations of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) that arise from divide-and-conquer algorithms.
importance: It gives a quick, systematic way to derive the complexity of recursive algorithms like merge sort.

title: Bit Manipulation Basics
definition: Bit manipulation uses bitwise operators to read, set, and toggle individual bits of integers for compact, fast computation.
importance: It enables constant-time set operations, flags, and many low-level optimizations.

title: Bit Masking
definition: Bit masking represents a subset of items as the bits of an integer, allowing subsets to be enumerated and combined with bitwise operations.
importance: It is the backbone of subset-based dynamic programming and state compression.

title: Bitwise Operations (AND, OR, XOR)
definition: AND, OR, XOR, and NOT operate on the individual bits of integers, forming the primitive building blocks of bit manipulation.
importance: These operations power masks, parity checks, and fast arithmetic tricks.

title: Euclidean Algorithm (GCD)
definition: The Euclidean algorithm computes the greatest common divisor of two integers by repeatedly replacing the larger number with its remainder modulo the smaller.
importance: GCD is fundamental to number theory, fractions, and modular arithmetic problems.

title: Sieve of Eratosthenes
definition: The Sieve of Eratosthenes finds all prime numbers up to N by iteratively marking the multiples of each prime as composite.
importance: It precomputes primes efficiently for number-theory and competitive-programming problems.

title: Fast Exponentiation
definition: Fast (binary) exponentiation computes a^b by squaring the base and halving the exponent, reducing the number of multiplications to logarithmic.
importance: It is essential for modular exponentiation in cryptography and combinatorics.

title: Extended Euclidean Algorithm
definition: The Extended Euclidean algorithm computes the GCD of two integers along with coefficients x, y satisfying ax + by = gcd(a, b).
importance: It is used to find modular multiplicative inverses and solve linear Diophantine equations.

title: Matrix Exponentiation
definition: Matrix exponentiation raises a transition matrix to a power using fast exponentiation to compute linear recurrences in logarithmic time.
importance: It computes terms of sequences like Fibonacci for astronomically large indices quickly.

title: Modular Arithmetic
definition: Modular arithmetic performs integer operations under a modulus, wrapping results into a fixed range to keep numbers bounded.
importance: It prevents overflow in large computations and underlies hashing and cryptography.

title: Permutations & Combinations
definition: Permutations count ordered arrangements and combinations count unordered selections of items from a set.
importance: They are core to counting problems, probability, and combinatorial DP.

title: Pascal's Triangle
definition: Pascal's Triangle is a triangular array where each entry is the sum of the two above it, giving the binomial coefficients.
importance: It provides an O(N^2) way to precompute combinations without division.

title: Probability Basics
definition: Probability basics quantify the likelihood of events on a scale from 0 to 1 using counting and expected-value reasoning.
importance: Probability underlies randomized algorithms and expected-time analysis.

title: Catalan Numbers
definition: Catalan numbers form a sequence counting many recursive structures such as balanced parentheses, binary trees, and triangulations.
importance: They appear frequently in combinatorial counting problems.

title: Pigeonhole Principle
definition: The pigeonhole principle states that if more items than containers are distributed, at least one container holds more than one item.
importance: It gives simple existence proofs used in many algorithmic arguments.

title: Floating Point Representation
definition: Floating-point representation encodes real numbers using a sign, mantissa, and exponent following the IEEE-754 standard.
importance: Knowing its limits helps avoid precision bugs in numeric code.

title: Memory Contiguity
definition: Memory contiguity refers to storing data in adjacent memory locations, which enables O(1) indexing and cache-friendly access.
importance: It explains why arrays are fast and why cache locality matters for performance.

title: Pointers & References
definition: Pointers and references store the memory address of a value, allowing indirect access and sharing of data between parts of a program.
importance: They are essential for building linked structures and understanding how data is passed.

title: Structs & Classes
definition: Structs and classes bundle related fields (and, for classes, behavior) into a single user-defined type.
importance: They are the foundation for modeling nodes, records, and objects in data structures.

title: Amortized Analysis
definition: Amortized analysis averages the cost of a sequence of operations to show that occasional expensive operations are cheap on average.
importance: It justifies the efficiency of dynamic arrays and other structures with rare costly steps.

title: Tail Recursion
definition: Tail recursion is a form of recursion where the recursive call is the last operation, allowing compilers to reuse the current stack frame.
importance: It lets some recursive algorithms run in constant stack space.

title: Bitwise Left & Right Shifts
definition: Left and right shift operators move the bits of an integer, effectively multiplying or dividing by powers of two.
importance: Shifts provide fast arithmetic and are building blocks of bit tricks.

title: Two's Complement Representation
definition: Two's complement is the standard way computers represent signed integers, where negatives are formed by inverting bits and adding one.
importance: It explains integer overflow behavior and how negative numbers work in hardware.

title: Recursion Trees
definition: A recursion tree visualizes the recursive calls of an algorithm as nodes, summing work per level to estimate total complexity.
importance: It is an intuitive way to derive the running time of recursive algorithms.

title: Logarithms in Computer Science
definition: Logarithms measure how many times a quantity can be divided by a base, and appear whenever a problem is repeatedly halved.
importance: They explain the efficiency of binary search, balanced trees, and divide-and-conquer.

title: Iterative vs Recursive Tradeoffs
definition: This topic compares iterative and recursive implementations in terms of readability, stack usage, and performance.
importance: Choosing the right style avoids stack overflows and clarifies code.

title: Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)
definition: The Fast Fourier Transform computes the discrete Fourier transform in O(N log N), enabling fast polynomial and big-integer multiplication.
importance: It is a cornerstone of signal processing and high-performance multiplication.

title: Modular Multiplicative Inverse
definition: The modular multiplicative inverse of a number a modulo m is a value x such that a·x ≡ 1 (mod m).
importance: It allows division under a modulus, which is needed in modular combinatorics.

title: Gray Code Mechanics
definition: Gray code is an ordering of binary numbers in which two successive values differ in exactly one bit.
importance: It minimizes transition errors in hardware and appears in combinatorial enumeration.

title: Chinese Remainder Theorem
definition: The Chinese Remainder Theorem reconstructs a number from its remainders modulo several pairwise-coprime moduli.
importance: It solves systems of congruences common in number-theory problems.

title: Euler's Totient Function
definition: Euler's totient function counts the integers up to N that are coprime with N.
importance: It powers Euler's theorem and modular-inverse computations in cryptography.

title: Array
definition: An array is a contiguous block of memory that stores elements of the same type, giving constant-time access by index.
importance: Arrays are the most fundamental data structure and underpin almost every other one.

title: Linked List
definition: A linked list stores elements in nodes where each node points to the next, allowing O(1) insertion and deletion at known positions.
importance: It teaches pointer manipulation and enables dynamic sequences without contiguous memory.

title: Doubly Linked List
definition: A doubly linked list is a linked list where each node has pointers to both its previous and next nodes, enabling bidirectional traversal.
importance: It allows O(1) deletion given a node and backs structures like LRU caches.

title: Circular Linked List
definition: A circular linked list links its last node back to the first, forming a loop with no natural end.
importance: It models round-robin scheduling and cyclic buffers.

title: Stack
definition: A stack is a last-in-first-out (LIFO) structure where elements are pushed and popped only from the top.
importance: Stacks power function calls, expression evaluation, and backtracking.

title: Stack (List-based)
definition: A list-based stack implements LIFO behavior using a linked list so that pushes and pops happen at the head node.
importance: It shows how the same abstract stack can be built without a fixed-size array.

title: Queue
definition: A queue is a first-in-first-out (FIFO) structure where elements are enqueued at the rear and dequeued from the front.
importance: Queues drive scheduling, buffering, and breadth-first search.

title: Queue (List-based)
definition: A list-based queue implements FIFO behavior using a linked list with pointers to both head and tail.
importance: It provides O(1) enqueue/dequeue without resizing an array.

title: Deque (Double Ended Queue)
definition: A deque is a double-ended queue that supports insertion and removal at both the front and the back in O(1).
importance: It underpins sliding-window algorithms and monotonic-queue techniques.

title: Circular Queue
definition: A circular queue is a fixed-size queue that reuses freed slots by wrapping the rear and front pointers around the array.
importance: It implements bounded buffers with no wasted space.

title: Monotonic Stack
definition: A monotonic stack keeps its elements in sorted (increasing or decreasing) order by popping violators as new elements arrive.
importance: It solves next-greater-element and histogram problems in linear time.

title: Monotonic Queue
definition: A monotonic queue maintains elements in sorted order within a deque to report the min or max of a sliding window in O(1).
importance: It is the key to O(N) sliding-window maximum problems.

title: Sparse Matrix
definition: A sparse matrix stores only its non-zero entries to save memory when most cells are zero.
importance: It makes large, mostly-empty grids and graphs practical to store.

title: Priority Queue
definition: A priority queue serves elements by priority rather than insertion order, typically backed by a heap.
importance: It is central to Dijkstra, scheduling, and greedy algorithms.

title: Skip List
definition: A skip list is a layered linked list with express lanes that allow O(log N) search, insertion, and deletion on average.
importance: It offers a simpler, randomized alternative to balanced trees.

title: Unrolled Linked List
definition: An unrolled linked list stores a small array of elements in each node, improving cache performance over a plain linked list.
importance: It blends array locality with linked-list flexibility.

title: Self-Organizing List
definition: A self-organizing list rearranges its elements based on access frequency so that frequently used items are found faster.
importance: It is a simple adaptive caching heuristic.

title: XOR Linked List
definition: An XOR linked list saves memory by storing the XOR of the previous and next node addresses in a single pointer field.
importance: It demonstrates a clever bit trick for memory-efficient doubly linked lists.

title: Dynamic Array Allocation
definition: A dynamic array grows automatically by allocating a larger buffer and copying elements when it runs out of capacity.
importance: It gives amortized O(1) append and backs list types in most languages.

title: Ring Buffer
definition: A ring buffer is a fixed-size circular array used as a FIFO buffer that overwrites or blocks when full.
importance: It is widely used for streaming data and producer-consumer pipelines.

title: Binary Tree
definition: A binary tree is a hierarchical structure where each node has at most two children, called left and right.
importance: It is the basis for search trees, heaps, and many recursive algorithms.

title: Binary Search Tree
definition: A binary search tree keeps smaller keys in the left subtree and larger keys in the right, enabling ordered O(log N) operations when balanced.
importance: It provides sorted storage with efficient search, insert, and delete.

title: AVL Tree
definition: An AVL tree is a self-balancing binary search tree that keeps subtree heights within one via rotations after each update.
importance: It guarantees O(log N) operations even in the worst case.

title: Red-Black Tree
definition: A red-black tree is a self-balancing BST that uses node colors and rotation rules to keep the tree approximately balanced.
importance: It backs many standard-library ordered maps and sets.

title: B-Tree
definition: A B-tree is a balanced multi-way search tree where each node holds many keys, minimizing disk reads for large datasets.
importance: It is the core structure behind databases and file systems.

title: B+ Tree
definition: A B+ tree is a B-tree variant that stores all values in the leaves and links leaves together for fast range scans.
importance: It is the dominant index structure in relational databases.

title: Trie (Prefix Tree)
definition: A trie stores strings by sharing common prefixes along tree edges, giving search time proportional to the key length.
importance: It powers autocomplete, spell-checking, and prefix queries.

title: Segment Tree
definition: A segment tree stores aggregate information over array ranges in a binary tree, allowing range queries and updates in O(log N).
importance: It answers range-sum/min/max queries with fast updates.

title: Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree)
definition: A Fenwick tree uses the binary representation of indices to compute prefix sums and point updates in O(log N) with little memory.
importance: It is a compact alternative to segment trees for prefix aggregates.

title: Heap (Priority Queue)
definition: A binary heap is a complete tree stored in an array where each parent dominates its children, giving O(log N) insert and extract.
importance: It is the standard implementation of a priority queue.

title: Max Heap
definition: A max heap is a binary heap in which every parent is greater than or equal to its children, so the maximum sits at the root.
importance: It provides constant-time access to the largest element for scheduling and heap sort.

title: Treap
definition: A treap combines a BST on keys with a heap on random priorities to stay balanced with high probability.
importance: It offers simple randomized balancing and supports split/merge.

title: Splay Tree
definition: A splay tree is a self-adjusting BST that moves each accessed node to the root, giving good amortized performance for skewed access.
importance: It provides cache-like behavior without extra bookkeeping.

title: K-D Tree
definition: A k-d tree partitions k-dimensional space by cycling through axes at each level to support nearest-neighbor and range queries.
importance: It accelerates spatial searches in graphics and machine learning.

title: Quad Tree
definition: A quad tree recursively subdivides a 2D region into four quadrants to index spatial data.
importance: It is used in image compression, collision detection, and maps.

title: Interval Tree
definition: An interval tree stores intervals so that all intervals overlapping a query point or range can be found efficiently.
importance: It answers overlap queries used in scheduling and computational geometry.

title: Ternary Search Tree
definition: A ternary search tree stores strings with three children per node (less, equal, greater), balancing trie speed with lower memory.
importance: It is a space-efficient structure for prefix and near-neighbor string search.

title: Binomial Heap
definition: A binomial heap is a collection of binomial trees that supports efficient merging of two heaps in O(log N).
importance: It is useful when frequent heap unions are required.

title: Fibonacci Heap
definition: A Fibonacci heap supports decrease-key and merge in O(1) amortized time, improving the asymptotics of some graph algorithms.
importance: It gives the best theoretical bounds for Dijkstra and Prim.

title: Cartesian Tree
definition: A Cartesian tree is a binary tree derived from a sequence that is heap-ordered by value and in-order equal to the sequence.
importance: It links range-minimum queries with lowest-common-ancestor problems.

title: Expression Tree
definition: An expression tree represents an arithmetic expression with operators at internal nodes and operands at leaves.
importance: It is used by compilers and calculators to evaluate expressions.

title: Threaded Binary Tree
definition: A threaded binary tree stores otherwise-null child pointers as links to in-order predecessors and successors for pointer-free traversal.
importance: It enables O(1)-space in-order traversal.

title: Radix Tree
definition: A radix tree (compressed trie) merges chains of single-child nodes to store strings compactly.
importance: It is used for IP routing tables and memory-efficient dictionaries.

title: Huffman Coding Tree
definition: A Huffman coding tree builds an optimal prefix code by repeatedly merging the two least frequent symbols.
importance: It is a classic greedy algorithm for lossless data compression.

title: AA Tree
definition: An AA tree is a simplified red-black tree variant that uses levels and only right-leaning links to ease implementation.
importance: It offers balanced-BST guarantees with simpler code.

title: Adjacency Matrix
definition: An adjacency matrix represents a graph as a 2D grid where cell (i, j) marks whether an edge exists between vertices i and j.
importance: It gives O(1) edge lookups and suits dense graphs.

title: Adjacency List
definition: An adjacency list stores, for each vertex, a list of its neighboring vertices, using space proportional to the number of edges.
importance: It is the memory-efficient representation for sparse graphs.

title: BFS
definition: Breadth-first search explores a graph level by level using a queue, visiting all neighbors before moving deeper.
importance: It finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs and explores connectivity.

title: DFS
definition: Depth-first search explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, using recursion or a stack.
importance: It underlies cycle detection, topological sort, and connectivity checks.

title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
definition: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths from a source in a graph with non-negative weights using a priority queue.
importance: It is the standard single-source shortest-path algorithm for routing.

title: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
definition: The Bellman-Ford algorithm computes shortest paths from a source and detects negative-weight cycles by relaxing all edges V-1 times.
importance: It handles negative edge weights that Dijkstra cannot.

title: Floyd-Warshall Algorithm
definition: The Floyd-Warshall algorithm computes shortest paths between all pairs of vertices via dynamic programming over intermediate nodes.
importance: It is a concise all-pairs shortest-path solution for small graphs.

title: Prim's MST
definition: Prim's algorithm grows a minimum spanning tree from a start vertex by repeatedly adding the cheapest edge that expands the tree.
importance: It builds least-cost networks and is a classic greedy algorithm.

title: Kruskal's MST
definition: Kruskal's algorithm builds a minimum spanning tree by adding edges in increasing weight order while avoiding cycles using a disjoint set.
importance: It efficiently constructs least-cost spanning trees on sparse graphs.

title: Topological Sort (Kahn's)
definition: Kahn's algorithm orders a directed acyclic graph by repeatedly removing vertices with no remaining incoming edges.
importance: It schedules tasks with dependencies and detects cycles.

title: Topological Sort (DFS-based)
definition: DFS-based topological sort orders a DAG by finishing depth-first traversal and pushing vertices onto a stack in reverse finish order.
importance: It provides an alternate, recursion-driven ordering of dependencies.

title: Disjoint Set Union (DSU)
definition: Disjoint Set Union tracks a partition of elements into groups, supporting near-constant-time union and find with path compression.
importance: It powers Kruskal, connectivity queries, and cycle detection.

title: Kosaraju's SCC
definition: Kosaraju's algorithm finds strongly connected components using two depth-first passes over the graph and its transpose.
importance: It reveals cyclic clusters in directed graphs.

title: Tarjan's SCC
definition: Tarjan's algorithm finds strongly connected components in a single DFS pass using discovery times and a stack.
importance: It computes SCCs more efficiently than two-pass approaches.

title: Eulerian Path/Circuit
definition: An Eulerian path visits every edge exactly once; an Eulerian circuit does so and returns to the start, existing under specific degree conditions.
importance: It solves route-planning problems like the Chinese postman.

title: Hamiltonian Path/Cycle
definition: A Hamiltonian path visits every vertex exactly once; finding one is an NP-complete problem usually solved by backtracking or bitmask DP.
importance: It models tour and sequencing problems with no known efficient solution.

title: Ford-Fulkerson (Max Flow)
definition: The Ford-Fulkerson method computes maximum flow by repeatedly pushing flow along augmenting paths until none remain.
importance: It solves network-flow and bipartite-matching problems.

title: Edmonds-Karp (Max Flow)
definition: Edmonds-Karp implements Ford-Fulkerson using BFS to find shortest augmenting paths, giving a polynomial time bound.
importance: It is a reliable, easy-to-implement max-flow algorithm.

title: Dinic's Algorithm
definition: Dinic's algorithm computes maximum flow faster by building level graphs with BFS and sending blocking flows with DFS.
importance: It handles large flow networks efficiently.

title: Hopcroft-Karp (Bipartite Matching)
definition: Hopcroft-Karp finds a maximum matching in a bipartite graph by augmenting along multiple shortest paths per phase.
importance: It is the fastest common algorithm for bipartite matching.

title: Johnson's Algorithm
definition: Johnson's algorithm finds all-pairs shortest paths on sparse graphs by reweighting edges with Bellman-Ford then running Dijkstra from each vertex.
importance: It beats Floyd-Warshall on sparse graphs with negative edges.

title: Bridges and Cut Vertices
definition: Bridges and cut vertices are edges and nodes whose removal disconnects a graph, found using DFS discovery and low-link values.
importance: They identify critical points in networks for reliability analysis.

title: 2-SAT Problem
definition: The 2-SAT problem decides satisfiability of boolean formulas with two literals per clause by finding SCCs of an implication graph.
importance: It solves constraint problems that reduce to boolean 2-clauses in linear time.

title: Heavy-Light Decomposition
definition: Heavy-light decomposition splits a tree into chains so that path queries can be answered with a segment tree in O(log^2 N).
importance: It enables efficient path updates and queries on trees.

title: Centroid Decomposition
definition: Centroid decomposition recursively splits a tree at its centroid to answer path and distance queries efficiently.
importance: It solves many tree path-counting problems in O(N log N).

title: Linear Search
definition: Linear search scans a collection element by element until it finds the target or exhausts the list.
importance: It is the simplest search and works on unsorted data.

title: Binary Search
definition: Binary search repeatedly halves a sorted range, discarding the half that cannot contain the target.
importance: It is the canonical O(log N) search and a template for many divide-and-conquer problems.

title: Bubble Sort
definition: Bubble sort repeatedly steps through the list, swapping adjacent out-of-order elements until no swaps are needed.
importance: It is a teaching sort that illustrates comparisons, swaps, and early termination.

title: Selection Sort
definition: Selection sort repeatedly selects the smallest remaining element and moves it to the sorted portion.
importance: It minimizes swaps and demonstrates in-place selection.

title: Insertion Sort
definition: Insertion sort builds a sorted prefix by inserting each new element into its correct position among the already-sorted items.
importance: It is efficient for small or nearly-sorted arrays and is stable.

title: Merge Sort
definition: Merge sort recursively divides the array in half, sorts each half, and merges the two sorted halves.
importance: It guarantees O(N log N) time and is a classic stable divide-and-conquer sort.

title: Quick Sort
definition: Quick sort partitions the array around a pivot so smaller elements precede it and larger follow, then recurses on each side.
importance: It is the fastest general-purpose in-place sort in practice.

title: Quick Sort (Hoare)
definition: Quick sort with Hoare's partition scheme uses two pointers moving inward from both ends, performing fewer swaps than Lomuto's scheme.
importance: It is a more efficient partition variant of quick sort.

title: Heap Sort
definition: Heap sort builds a max heap from the array and repeatedly extracts the maximum to produce a sorted order in place.
importance: It offers guaranteed O(N log N) time without extra memory.

title: Counting Sort
definition: Counting sort orders integers within a small range by counting occurrences and computing prefix positions, without comparisons.
importance: It sorts bounded-range integers in linear time.

title: Radix Sort
definition: Radix sort sorts numbers digit by digit using a stable sort like counting sort for each digit position.
importance: It sorts fixed-width integers or strings in linear time.

title: Bucket Sort
definition: Bucket sort distributes elements into buckets, sorts each bucket, then concatenates them.
importance: It is fast when input is uniformly distributed.

title: Shell Sort
definition: Shell sort generalizes insertion sort by comparing elements separated by a gap that shrinks each pass.
importance: It improves on insertion sort for medium-sized arrays.

title: Comb Sort
definition: Comb sort improves bubble sort by comparing elements a shrinking gap apart to eliminate small values near the end quickly.
importance: It removes "turtles" that slow bubble sort.

title: Pigeonhole Sort
definition: Pigeonhole sort places each element into a hole indexed by its key, then reads the holes in order.
importance: It sorts data whose key range is close to the element count.

title: Cycle Sort
definition: Cycle sort minimizes the number of writes by rotating each element directly into its final position along a cycle.
importance: It is optimal when write operations are costly.

title: Odd-Even Sort
definition: Odd-even (brick) sort repeatedly compares and swaps odd- and even-indexed adjacent pairs, suiting parallel hardware.
importance: It is a simple parallelizable variant of bubble sort.

title: Ternary Search
definition: Ternary search splits a range into three parts to locate the extremum of a unimodal function.
importance: It optimizes unimodal functions where binary search does not apply.

title: Exponential Search
definition: Exponential search finds a range by doubling an index until it passes the target, then binary searches within that range.
importance: It is ideal for unbounded or infinite sorted sequences.

title: Interpolation Search
definition: Interpolation search estimates the target position using linear interpolation on uniformly distributed sorted data.
importance: It can beat binary search on evenly spread keys.

title: Two Pointers
definition: The two-pointer technique uses two indices moving through a sequence to solve pair, window, or partition problems in linear time.
importance: It turns many O(N^2) array problems into O(N) solutions.

title: Sliding Window
definition: The sliding-window technique maintains a moving contiguous range and updates an aggregate incrementally as the window shifts.
importance: It solves subarray and substring problems in linear time.

title: Fast and Slow Pointers
definition: The fast-and-slow-pointer technique advances two pointers at different speeds to detect cycles or find midpoints.
importance: It detects linked-list cycles and midpoints in O(1) space (Floyd's algorithm).

title: Backtracking
definition: Backtracking incrementally builds candidate solutions and abandons a branch as soon as it cannot lead to a valid result.
importance: It systematically explores combinatorial search spaces like permutations and Sudoku.

title: Divide and Conquer
definition: Divide and conquer breaks a problem into independent subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines their results.
importance: It is the paradigm behind merge sort, quick sort, and many fast algorithms.

title: Greedy Algorithms
definition: A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step in the hope of reaching a global optimum.
importance: It solves scheduling, MST, and coin problems when the greedy-choice property holds.

title: Dynamic Programming
definition: Dynamic programming solves problems by combining solutions to overlapping subproblems, caching results to avoid recomputation.
importance: It is one of the most important and heavily tested algorithmic techniques.

title: Hash Table
definition: A hash table maps keys to buckets via a hash function, giving average O(1) insertion, deletion, and lookup.
importance: It is the go-to structure for fast key-value storage and deduplication.

title: Bitmask DP
definition: Bitmask DP encodes a set of chosen items as the bits of an integer to iterate over subsets as DP states.
importance: It solves problems over small sets like the traveling salesman.

title: Digit DP
definition: Digit DP counts numbers in a range that satisfy digit-based constraints by processing digits with tight/loose state.
importance: It answers counting queries over numeric ranges efficiently.

title: Interval DP
definition: Interval DP builds answers for larger sub-intervals from smaller ones, often splitting at an internal point.
importance: It solves matrix-chain and optimal-BST style problems.

title: LCS & Edit Distance Patterns
definition: These DP patterns compare two sequences to compute the longest common subsequence or minimum edit distance between them.
importance: They power diff tools, spell-checkers, and DNA alignment.

title: Knapsack 0/1 & Unbounded
definition: Knapsack DP selects items with weights and values to maximize value under a capacity, either using each item once (0/1) or unlimited (unbounded).
importance: It is the archetypal resource-allocation DP.

title: Game Theory (Minimax)
definition: Minimax evaluates two-player zero-sum games by assuming each player plays optimally, minimizing the maximum possible loss.
importance: It underlies AI for board games and adversarial search.

title: Segment Tree with Lazy Propagation
definition: Lazy propagation defers range updates in a segment tree until needed, allowing O(log N) range updates and queries.
importance: It supports efficient range-update range-query workloads.

title: Meet in the Middle
definition: Meet in the middle splits the input in half, enumerates each half, and combines the halves to halve the exponent of brute force.
importance: It makes some exponential problems tractable.

title: Square Root Decomposition
definition: Square-root decomposition splits an array into blocks of size √N to answer range queries and updates in O(√N).
importance: It is a simple alternative to segment trees for range queries.

title: Mo's Algorithm
definition: Mo's algorithm answers offline range queries efficiently by reordering them and moving two pointers to add/remove elements.
importance: It handles many range queries without heavy data structures.

title: KMP String Matching
definition: The Knuth-Morris-Pratt algorithm searches for a pattern in text in linear time using a precomputed prefix-function table.
importance: It matches strings without re-examining characters.

title: Rabin-Karp String Matching
definition: Rabin-Karp uses rolling hashes to compare a pattern against substrings of the text quickly.
importance: It efficiently finds multiple patterns and substrings via hashing.

title: Z-Algorithm
definition: The Z-algorithm computes, for each position, the length of the longest substring matching the prefix, enabling linear pattern search.
importance: It is a concise linear-time string-matching tool.

title: Aho-Corasick Automaton
definition: Aho-Corasick builds a trie with failure links to match many patterns against a text simultaneously in linear time.
importance: It powers multi-keyword search and intrusion detection.

title: Manacher's Algorithm
definition: Manacher's algorithm finds the longest palindromic substring in linear time by reusing previously computed palindrome radii.
importance: It solves palindrome problems faster than the O(N^2) approach.

title: Sweep Line Algorithm
definition: A sweep-line algorithm processes geometric events in sorted order as an imaginary line moves across the plane.
importance: It solves interval-overlap and segment-intersection problems.

title: Convex Hull (Graham Scan)
definition: Graham scan computes the convex hull of a point set by sorting points by angle and maintaining a stack of hull vertices.
importance: It is a foundational computational-geometry algorithm.
